Name: Alameda
Recognition: Michelin one star (awarded March 2026 — Faro's first ever)
What_to_order: the tasting menu — Origami (12 courses) or Umami (8 courses) — the kitchen reads the Ria, oyster with eucalyptus ponzu and lime, octopus from Olhão, smoked eel
Notes: Chef Rui Sequeira, ex-Ocean (Hans Neuner) and direct alumnus of the Vila Vita kitchen. The star reads as a line drawn under three decades of Algarve being only resort cooking. Thu–Mon dinner, Sunday lunch slots.

Name: CHECKin by Leonel Pereira
What_to_order: the seasonal menu — Check-in / Cozinhados no Tacho / Momentos Especiais / Check-out; ask the sommelier for niche Portuguese producers
Notes: Leonel Pereira held a star at São Gabriel (Almancil), gave it up to open a more democratic concept inside the walls. 26 seats indoors + 30-seat terrace. Wine list among the most curious in the city.

Name: Estaminé — Ilha Deserta
Recognition: The cult lunch of southern Portugal — solar-powered restaurant on an uninhabited barrier island
What_to_order: whatever the Ria gave that morning — oysters, grilled sea bream, conquilhas, arroz de lingueirão; the tuna and octopus estupetas
Notes: Animaris ferry from the marina (~30 min through the lagoon, return ~€10). Rebuilt August 2021 after a March fire. Photovoltaic plant covers all energy; seawater desalination for drinking water. First boat out, last boat back.

Name: Tasca do Ricky
Recognition: The canonical Faro tasca — organic ingredients, ancestral recipes, no more than four per table
What_to_order: cataplana de marisco, arroz de lingueirão, amêijoas à bulhão pato, whatever's on the chalkboard
Notes: Mon–Sat 19:00–22:00; closed Sundays. The cataplana here is repeatedly cited as the best in the city. The waiter knows what you should order better than you do.

Name: Faz Gostos
Recognition: Michelin Plate — local gastronomic reference inside the walls
What_to_order: arroz de marisco (≈ €40 for two), cataplana, the day's fish
Notes: Established 2009. Two halls (up to 150 covers — unusually large for Michelin-listed but the consistency holds). 18th-c. underground wine cellar. Modern Algarve cooking that opens the Cidade Velha to serious eaters.

Name: Vila Adentro
Recognition: Azulejo-tiled 18th-c. building on the central walled square
What_to_order: vegetable cataplana (proof the copper pot works without shellfish), octopus, prawn-curry rice
Notes: Open 10:00–22:30 daily — one of the rare all-day options inside the walls. Vegan-friendly without being only that.

Name: Tertúlia Algarvia
Recognition: Traditional Algarve dishes plus cooking classes — the place to learn cataplana technique
What_to_order: seasonal menu tied to what the land and sea give that week; sign up for the class
Notes: Regional-products focus. The cooking class makes it more useful than another menu.

Name: Adega Nova
Recognition: Converted tile-and-brick warehouse, open 11:00–23:00 daily, an extensive Dão / Douro list
What_to_order: grilled fish, arroz de marisco, choco frito; two with a bottle around €40
Notes: Since 1986. The value benchmark of central Faro — honest portions, real fish, real wine.

Name: A Venda
Recognition: Petiscos in a grandmother's lounge — open kitchen, sharing plates
What_to_order: pork cheeks, marinated vegetables, local cheese board, anything off the daily list
Notes: Tue–Sat 13:00-15:00 and 19:30-23:00. Vegan-, vegetarian- and meat-eater-friendly without forcing the joke.

Name: A Do Pinto
What_to_order: cataplana with shrimp and clams, grilled fish
Notes: Word-of-mouth Faro address. Not for tourists — which is exactly why you go.

Name: Mercado de Olhão
Recognition: Two red-brick halls on the waterfront — the Algarve's largest fish market
What_to_order: Saturday before 09:00: sardines (~€4/kg) off the boats, octopus, Pacific oysters, amêijoa-boa, Ria Formosa salt and flor de sal
Notes: Built 1912–1916, inaugurated 1916. Two halls — east is fish, west is fruit/vegetables. Neo-Arabic green domes, 20th-c. azulejo panels of Algarve fishing life inside. Saturday is the experience.

Name: Mercado Municipal de Faro
Recognition: Faro's city market — fish, vegetables, the city's reference bifana inside
What_to_order: bifana from Snack Bar da Xica; daily fish; fruit and bread
Notes: Less theatre than Olhão; more utility. Go early. Snack Bar da Xica's bifana is repeatedly cited as the city's best.

Name: Bago Wine Bar
Recognition: Faro's natural-wine reference — ~180 Portuguese references, 30–50 natural / organic / biodynamic
What_to_order: by-the-glass tour of the Algarve list; tiborna open-toasts, cheese, charcuterie, fish tapas, roast black-pork sausage
Notes: 10:00-23:00, closed Sun–Mon. Live music most Fridays from 19:30. Raisin-listed.

Name: Hotel Faro Rooftop
Recognition: The best rooftop view in the city — harbour, Ria Formosa, the barrier islands
Notes: Touristy but the view actually pays. Go at sunset, leave before the music.

Name: Morgado do Quintão
Recognition: Family-owned since 1810; Algarve's largest certified-organic vineyard; consultant Joana Maçanita
What_to_order: monovarietal Crato Branco (the canonical Algarve white); Negra Mole rosé under the 2,000-year-old olive tree; farmers' table lunch pairing family recipes with the wines
Notes: Mon–Sat 16:00 visit slots. Won "Best Wine in Portugal – Algarve" (Revista de Vinhos) two years running. Farmstay on the estate.

Name: Vila Joya
Recognition: Michelin two stars — held since 1999, the longest-running ★★ on the Algarve coast
What_to_order: the daily-changing tasting; no dish appears twice across the year
Notes: Austrian chef Dieter Koschina, in the Algarve since 1991; first star 1995, second 1999 — the first ★★ in Portugal. 30 seats. Cliffside Atlantic terrace.

Name: Ocean Restaurant
Recognition: Michelin two stars since 2011; Best Service Award 2026
What_to_order: the discovery menu — Atlantic fish read through Portugal's Age of Discovery routes
Notes: Chef Hans Neuner (Portugal's Chef of the Year 2009 and 2012). Rui Sequeira of Alameda passed through this kitchen — the lineage is direct.

Name: Bon Bon
What_to_order: the Essence menu — a tightly edited celebration of Algarve biodiversity
Notes: Chef José Lopes (succeeding Louis Anjos in the 2026 guide). Less resort, more personal vision than Vila Joya or Ocean.
